title: How AI Sees Your World

modules.title: What Is Computer Vision, Really
modules.sub: Unpacking how machines learn to interpret visual information
modules.description: Learners explore the foundational mechanics behind computer vision, from pixels to pattern recognition. They examine how training data and neural networks enable machines to 'see,' building a working mental model they will carry through the rest of the course.

modules.title: Cameras That Recognize Faces and Objects
modules.sub: How facial and object recognition work in daily life
modules.description: Learners investigate the technology behind face unlock, photo tagging, and object detection systems they encounter every day. They assess how these systems make identification decisions and what factors affect their accuracy and reliability.

modules.title: Computer Vision in Retail and Payments
modules.sub: Visual AI reshaping how we shop and pay
modules.description: Learners examine how retailers use computer vision for cashierless checkout, inventory tracking, and customer behavior analysis. They weigh the trade-offs between convenience and the data being collected about consumers in physical spaces.

modules.title: Medical Imaging and Diagnostic AI
modules.sub: When AI assists doctors in reading scans and images
modules.description: Learners explore how computer vision is applied to X-rays, MRIs, and pathology slides to detect disease earlier and more consistently. They consider the ethical and practical questions around trust, liability, and human oversight in clinical settings.

modules.title: Self-Driving Cars and Visual AI
modules.sub: How vehicles use vision to navigate a complex world
modules.description: Learners map out how autonomous vehicles process camera feeds, lidar, and sensor fusion to make real-time driving decisions. They evaluate what happens when visual AI fails and who bears responsibility when those failures have physical consequences.

modules.title: Surveillance, Privacy, and Public Spaces
modules.sub: Understanding who is watching, why, and with what authority
modules.description: Learners analyze how governments and private entities deploy computer vision in streets, transit, and workplaces. They engage with legal frameworks, civil liberties arguments, and the question of what counts as reasonable monitoring in a democratic society.

modules.title: Bias and Errors in Visual AI
modules.sub: Exploring how visual systems fail unevenly across groups
modules.description: Learners investigate documented cases where computer vision systems produced higher error rates for certain demographics, particularly based on skin tone, gender, and age. They critically examine how biased training data and design choices translate into real-world harm.

modules.title: Building a More Accountable Visual AI
modules.sub: Identifying what governance and design reforms can change
modules.description: Learners synthesize everything from previous modules to evaluate proposed technical, policy, and organizational solutions for making visual AI fairer and more transparent. They leave with a framework for assessing accountability claims made by developers, regulators, and deployers of these systems.

synopsis: From unlocking your phone with your face to helping doctors spot tumors, computer vision is quietly embedded in dozens of everyday technologies most people never think twice about. This course demystifies how machines interpret images and video, and what that means for your privacy, safety, and rights.
rationale: Computer vision is one of the most pervasive and consequential AI application domains, yet most people encounter it daily without understanding how it works, who controls it, or how to challenge its misuse.
